title: Routing
description: File-based routing, layouts, and SPA navigation

blocks.text: Page files live in client/pages/. The file path maps directly to the URL.
A page component receives ctx as its argument, which is the full request context including state, params, url, and all response helpers.

The router tolerates trailing-slash mismatches automatically. A route registered as /wissen matches both /wissen and /wissen/, and vice versa — no 404 either way. Exact matches always take priority when both forms are registered.
Use the trailingSlashes() middleware (always | never) when you want to canonicalise URLs for SEO/analytics or force a 308 redirect. Without it, both forms simply work.

File-based middleware (_middleware.ts) applies to its directory and below — and it correctly matches routes that contain optional parameters. A middleware at pages/api/_middleware.ts will run for both pages/api/[[id]].tsx and pages/api/users/[id].tsx, even though the first route's URL pattern includes an optional group.

Internally, the router strips optional {/:param}? groups before splitting a route into segments, so /api{/:opt}?/endpoint segments identically to /api/endpoint. Without this, middleware registered at /api would skip routes with optional params under it.
Wrap a segment name in brackets to make it dynamic. Access captured values via ctx.params.
Use [...slug] for catch-all routes that match any depth.
Use [[slug]] (double brackets) for an optional segment. The same file matches both the bare path and any single value:
Optional params arrive as an empty string when absent. Treat falsy as the no-arg case (`name || fallback`) rather than checking for undefined.
A _layout.tsx file wraps all pages in its directory and below. Layouts receive a Component prop that renders the child page.
Layouts nest — pages/docs/_layout.tsx applies only to /docs/* routes and stacks on top of pages/_layout.tsx.
Layouts can be async, letting you fetch data for the shell (e.g., navigation items from a database) before the page renders.

Routes registered programmatically via app.route(), app.get(), app.post(), app.ws() etc. fully participate in the layout tree — including the index route at the layout's path. A layout at /foo applies to both /foo and /foo/bar.

If you've been mixing app.layout() with app.route() and noticed the index page rendering without its layout, that bug is fixed. Routes attach to the deepest matching segment so layouts at the same path apply correctly.
_app.tsx provides the outer HTML shell — the <html>, <head>, and <body> tags. Every page is rendered inside it.
The f-client-nav attribute enables SPA navigation. Without it, every link click triggers a full page reload.
When f-client-nav is set on <body>, Howl intercepts link clicks and fetches only the <Partial> region that changed — no full page reloads.
Navigation triggers a GET request with a special header. The server detects this and returns only the partial HTML fragment, which the client swaps into the DOM.
Partials are identified by name. Wrap any region you want to update independently:

<Partial name="sidebar">
  <Sidebar items={items} />
</Partial>
Average SPA navigation in Howl takes 5-10ms on a local server. The single-pass SSR render and Link preload headers keep it fast.

Forms inside an f-client-nav body are only intercepted for partial submission when they explicitly opt in. A form opts in by setting one of: an f-partial attribute, an action attribute, or having a submit button with formaction. Otherwise, the form submits normally with a full page reload — even though the surrounding body has f-client-nav.

// Intercepted — explicit `action` opts the form into partial submission
<form action="/api/save" method="post">
  <input name="title" />
  <button>Save</button>
</form>

// Not intercepted — no action/f-partial means the form does a normal submit
<form method="post">
  <input name="title" />
  <button>Save</button>
</form>
If you want partial submission without a server endpoint different from the current page, set f-partial="" explicitly: <form f-partial method="post">…</form>.

ctx.redirect() automatically preserves the partial-nav search param when called from a partial request. The redirected page renders in partial mode and the SPA stays in partial-nav flow — no manual threading required.

ctx.partialRedirect() has been removed. Earlier releases shipped it as a thin wrapper around ctx.redirect(); ctx.redirect() now does the partial-nav preservation natively, and the alias was deprecated for several releases before removal.

When a link inside f-client-nav points to a resource that returns a non-HTML Content-Type — a file download, an image, a Content-Disposition: attachment response, etc. — the SPA detects the non-HTML response and falls back to a full browser navigation. The browser lands on the raw response, just like a non-SPA page would.
API routes are not the intended target for <a href> — use fetch() for those. The same fallback applies if you accidentally link to one, but it should not be part of your design.

Howl marks active links automatically by stamping data-current / data-ancestor and aria-current attributes during render and partial swaps. Style your nav with the data-* attributes — no extra logic in components.

Query parameters are part of the match. A link with query params is only marked Current when the URL's search string matches exactly — otherwise it's demoted to Ancestor. Links without query params still match any current URL, which is useful for an "All" tab.
// Visiting /tabs?sort=name All // → data-current (no query, matches anything) By name // → data-current (search matches) By price // → data-ancestor (different search)
Setting your own aria-current is respected. Component libraries like daisyUI tabs that manage active state via aria-current keep working — Howl will not stomp on a user-set value.

Non-executable script tags in the page head — like application/ld+json for SEO structured data — are appended to document.head during partial navigation, with deduplication so they don't accumulate across repeat navigations. Executable script types (module / text/javascript / importmap) are skipped to prevent unintended re-execution.
Give your data scripts an id when possible — Howl uses it to update an existing script in place. Without an id, deduplication falls back to comparing textContent.
